title: TRIAD-Audit: An Audited Evaluation of Route Labels on Context-Memory Conflict Benchmarks
description: TRIAD-Audit is a narrowly scoped audited evaluation study layered on top of existing conflict benchmarks, especially CUB and WhoQA. Instead of proposing a new benchmark or router, it tests whether human-audited `context`/`memory`/`abstain` labels and alias-aware normalization materially change policy rankings and deployment conclusions.
motivation: The conflict-benchmark space is already crowded by CUB, Who’s Who, ConflictBank, ClashEval, and related studies, so a broad novelty claim is not defensible. The remaining gap is evaluative: existing benchmarks usually do not provide a human-audited item-level oracle for the correct action among using context, relying on memory, or abstaining, and benchmark rankings may therefore be sensitive to label policy, alias handling, and the memory-versus-abstain boundary.
proposed_approach: Audit a fixed 320-item subset drawn from CUB and WhoQA, with an explicit 80-item dev split and 240-item locked test split. Publish route-label guidelines, final human labels, adjudication notes, and alias sets; rank simple fixed policies under original benchmark scoring, automatic proxy route labels, and audited TRIAD scoring; and pre-register ranking-instability, agreement, and coverage thresholds so the main claim stands or fails on a narrow evaluation question.

related_work.title: CUB: Benchmarking Context Utilisation Techniques for Language Models
related_work.authors: Lovisa Hagström, Youna Kim, Haeun Yu, Sang-goo Lee, Richard Johansson, Hyunsoo Cho, Isabelle Augenstein
related_work.how_it_differs: CUB is the closest prior art and already benchmarks context-utilization methods under gold, conflicting, and irrelevant contexts. TRIAD-Audit does not replace it; it audits whether CUB-style ranking conclusions change when the oracle explicitly labels the normative action as context, memory, or abstain.

related_work.title: Who’s Who: Large Language Models Meet Knowledge Conflicts in Practice
related_work.authors: Quang Hieu Pham, Hoang Ngo, Anh Tuan Luu, Dat Quoc Nguyen
related_work.how_it_differs: WhoQA contributes realistic same-name conflict cases that stress alias resolution and ambiguity. TRIAD-Audit uses this setting to audit the memory-versus-abstain boundary rather than proposing another ambiguity benchmark.

related_work.title: ConflictBank: A Benchmark for Evaluating the Influence of Knowledge Conflicts in LLM
related_work.authors: Zhaochen Su, Jun Zhang, Xiaoye Qu, Tong Zhu, Yanshu Li, Jiashuo Sun, Juntao Li, Min Zhang, Yu Cheng
related_work.how_it_differs: ConflictBank is a large benchmark-construction effort spanning retrieved conflicts, embedded conflicts, and their interaction. TRIAD-Audit is much narrower: a small audited evaluation study asking whether benchmark rankings are label-sensitive.

related_work.title: ClashEval: Quantifying the tug-of-war between an LLM's internal prior and external evidence
related_work.authors: Kevin Wu, Eric Wu, James Zou
related_work.how_it_differs: ClashEval descriptively measures when models follow retrieved evidence versus their prior under controlled perturbations. TRIAD-Audit instead provides a normative audited oracle for what the model should have done on existing benchmark items.

related_work.title: Studying Large Language Model Behaviors Under Context-Memory Conflicts With Real Documents
related_work.authors: Evgenii Kortukov, Alexander Rubinstein, Elisa Nguyen, Seong Joon Oh
related_work.how_it_differs: This work shows that realistic document conflicts behave differently from synthetic ones, motivating careful evaluation. TRIAD-Audit builds on that motivation by auditing item-level action labels rather than introducing another conflict dataset.

related_work.title: Adaptive Chameleon or Stubborn Sloth: Revealing the Behavior of Large Language Models in Knowledge Conflicts
related_work.authors: Jian Xie, Kai Zhang, Jiangjie Chen, Renze Lou, Yu Su
related_work.how_it_differs: This paper analyzes model behavior under knowledge conflicts but does not center its contribution on adjudicated route labels or ranking sensitivity under different evaluation oracles.

related_work.title: Task Matters: Knowledge Requirements Shape LLM Responses to Context-Memory Conflict
related_work.authors: Kaiser Sun, Fan Bai, Mark Dredze
related_work.how_it_differs: This paper supports the idea that the right source depends on task semantics. TRIAD-Audit operationalizes that insight as a small audited evaluation protocol with explicit action labels and pre-registered stability thresholds.

hypothesis: On existing conflict benchmarks such as CUB and WhoQA, human-audited route labels and alias-aware normalization will materially change policy rankings or deployment recommendations relative to original benchmark scoring or automatic proxy route labels.
success_criteria: The audited subset has a fixed and internally consistent design: 320 total labeled items, an 80-item audited dev split, and a 240-item locked audited test split used for the main ranking analysis.
Audit reliability is credible, with overall Cohen's kappa of at least 0.70 on double-labeled items and at least 0.60 on the harder memory-versus-abstain boundary.
Audit coverage is adequate, with at least 95% of sampled items receiving a final non-discarded audited label.

Ranking instability is substantial enough to support the main claim: either the top-ranked policy changes, or there are at least two pairwise ranking reversals with Kendall tau at or below 0.70 between original and audited rankings.
Automatic proxy labels disagree with final audited labels often enough to matter in practice, affecting at least 12% of locked-test items or altering a deployment recommendation.
